# RushDB Docs

## Docs

- [Agent Memory Model](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/agent-memory-model): RushDB is designed from the ground up as a structured memory store for AI agents. Unlike flat vector databases or document stores, RushDB gives agents three distinct memory layers — each addressing a different dimension of knowledge — and a retrieval stack that composes them at query time.
- [Billing Model](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/billing-model): Overview
- [Bring Your Own Vectors (BYOV)](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/bring-your-own-vectors): By default, RushDB generates embeddings server-side when you create an embedding index on a string property. With BYOV (Bring Your Own Vectors) you compute the embeddings yourself and push them alongside your records. RushDB stores, indexes, and searches them — you stay in full control of the model and the pipeline.
- [Data Ingestion](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/data-ingestion): RushDB accepts raw data — JSON objects, nested trees, flat arrays, or CSV — and turns it into a fully typed, linked graph. No schema definitions, no migrations, no manual relationship wiring.
- [Main Concepts](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/index): The fundamental ideas behind RushDB — how it stores, links, and queries your data.
- [Knowledge Units (KU)](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/knowledge-units): What is a Knowledge Unit?
- [Labels](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/labels): Labels in RushDB are an essential part of the database schema, providing a way to categorize and organize records.
- [Ontology & Schema Discovery](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/ontology-schema-discovery): The Ontology API returns a live, computed snapshot of what exists in your RushDB project: every label, every property per label (with its type and value distribution), and the full relationship map between labels. Agents use it to bootstrap schema awareness at the start of a session — no hardcoded schema, no external documentation required.
- [Properties](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/properties): The fundamental unit of meaningful data in RushDB is known as a Property. Properties are first-class citizens in the RushDB architecture and serve as critical links that interconnect diverse data within Records across the graph database.
- [Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/records): In RushDB, Records are fundamental data structures that store meaningful key-value data. Each Record consists of individual properties (key-value pairs) and can be connected to other Records through relationships.
- [Relationships](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/relationships): In RushDB, relationships are the connections that link Records together, creating a powerful graph structure that represents both the data itself and how different pieces of data relate to one another. These connections enable intuitive data modeling that aligns with how we naturally think about information and its associations.
- [Grouping Search Results (groupBy)](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/search/group-by): The groupBy option in a SearchQuery lets you pivot, summarize, and compute metrics on records instead of returning a raw list. It works together with the select clause. If no select expressions are provided, groupBy is ignored. The legacy aggregate clause is deprecated and should only be used for vector similarity until select supports it.
- [Introduction](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/search/introduction): RushDB provides a powerful and flexible search system that allows you to efficiently query and traverse your graph data. The Search API is a cornerstone of RushDB, enabling you to find records, filter by conditions, navigate relationships, compute metrics, and format the returned data exactly as needed. The legacy aggregate clause is deprecated and should only be used for vector similarity until select supports it.
- [Labels](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/search/labels): The labels property in SearchQuery allows you to filter records by their label types. Labels in RushDB are categories or classifications assigned to records that help organize and identify different types of data.
- [Pagination and Order](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/search/pagination-order): SearchQuery provides flexible pagination and ordering capabilities to control the volume of returned data and the sequence in which records are presented.
- [Select Expressions](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/search/select): The select clause shapes the output of a SearchQuery: compute metrics, derive new metrics from other outputs, collect related records as arrays, and bucket datetime fields into calendar intervals — all in a single declarative map. The legacy aggregate clause is deprecated and should only be used for vector similarity until select supports it.
- [Where](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/search/where): The where clause in SearchQuery is a powerful mechanism to filter records based on property values and relationships. It's one of the key elements that make RushDB queries flexible and expressive.
- [Semantic Search](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/semantic-search): RushDB lets you search records by meaning, not just exact field values. Create an embedding index on any string property and every record that carries that property becomes searchable by natural-language similarity — while still supporting all the usual field filters, pagination, and graph traversal.
- [Storage](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/storage): RushDB uses a dual storage architecture:
- [Transactions](https://docs.rushdb.com/concepts/transactions): In RushDB, Transactions provide a mechanism to group multiple database operations into a single atomic unit of work. They ensure data consistency by guaranteeing that either all operations within the transaction succeed, or none of them do.
- [Get API Key](https://docs.rushdb.com/get-started/get-api-key): Learn how to get your RushDB API token
- [Quick Start](https://docs.rushdb.com/get-started/quick-tutorial): The fastest path to RushDB — pick your preferred tool.
- [Introduction](https://docs.rushdb.com/index): 
- [Configuration](https://docs.rushdb.com/mcp-server/configuration): Connection modes
- [Examples](https://docs.rushdb.com/mcp-server/examples): Discovery
- [MCP Server for RushDB](https://docs.rushdb.com/mcp-server/introduction): Connect Claude, ChatGPT, Cursor, or any MCP-compatible client to your RushDB database. Type natural language; the server handles discovery, query building, and execution.
- [Quickstart](https://docs.rushdb.com/mcp-server/quickstart): Two ways to connect:
- [Tools](https://docs.rushdb.com/mcp-server/tools): The RushDB MCP server exposes a comprehensive set of tools. Each tool includes a name, description, inputSchema, outputSchema, and client-facing annotations. Tool calls return both human-readable text and structured MCP structuredContent, so clients can render or reuse returned records, totals, CSV exports, ontology data, and operation metadata without parsing text.
- [Troubleshooting](https://docs.rushdb.com/mcp-server/troubleshooting): HTTP errors
- [Advanced Indexing — BYOV](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/ai/advanced-indexing): External indexes (BYOV — Bring Your Own Vectors) let you supply pre-computed embedding vectors instead of having the server compute them. Use them when you need:
- [Embedding Indexes](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/ai/indexing): An embedding index is a policy that tells RushDB to vectorize a specific string property for a label. Once status is ready, every record matching that label+property pair is searchable via db.ai.search().
- [Overview](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/ai/overview): RushDB is a self-aware memory layer for agents, humans, and apps. It continuously understands its own structure — labels, fields, value distributions, relationships — and exposes that knowledge so agents can reason over real data without hallucinating schema details, and apps can retrieve semantically relevant context on demand.
- [Semantic Search](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/ai/search): db.ai.search() performs semantic vector search across records that have an associated embedding index.
- [Writing Records with Vectors](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/ai/write-with-vectors): RushDB lets you attach pre-computed embedding vectors to records at write time, eliminating the need for a separate db.ai.indexes.upsert_vectors() call. Any operation that creates or modifies records supports this through the vectors parameter.
- [Introduction](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/introduction): Push JSON, query by value or meaning, traverse graphs — from Python.
- [Labels](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/labels): db.labels.find() accepts a standard SearchQuery where clause to filter labels by the fields of their records. It returns all labels that match regardless of how many records satisfy the condition.
- [Properties](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/properties): Find Properties
- [Record](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/python-reference/record): The Record class represents a record in RushDB and provides methods for manipulating individual records, including updates, relationships, and deletions.
- [SearchResult](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/python-reference/search-result): The SearchResult class is a container for search results that follows Python SDK best practices. It provides both list-like access and iteration support, along with metadata about the search operation including total count and pagination information.
- [SearchQuery](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/python-reference/SearchQuery): SearchQuery is a dictionary type that defines the structure for querying records in RushDB. It provides a flexible way to filter, sort, paginate, and shape results. For more information on search concepts, see the search documentation.
- [Transaction](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/python-reference/transaction): The Transaction class represents a record in RushDB and provides methods for manipulating individual records, including updates, relationships, and deletions.
- [Raw Queries](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/raw-queries): This endpoint is only available when your project is connected to your own Neo4j database. Connecting a custom Neo4j instance is available on the free tier — see the RushDB dashboard to set it up.
- [Create Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/records/create-records): Three methods for writing records. For nested/graph data see Import Data.
- [Delete Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/records/delete-records): Delete by ID
- [Get Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/records/get-records): Find by ID
- [Import Data](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/records/import-data): Pass nested dicts — RushDB walks the structure and links each level as a related record.
- [Update Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/records/update-records): Partial Update
- [Relationships](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/relationships): Attach
- [Transactions](https://docs.rushdb.com/python-sdk/transactions): Group writes atomically — all succeed or all roll back.
- [Advanced Indexing — BYOV](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/ai/advanced-indexing): External indexes (BYOV — Bring Your Own Vectors) let you supply pre-computed embedding vectors instead of having the server compute them. Use them when you need:
- [Embedding Indexes](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/ai/indexing): An embedding index is a policy that tells RushDB to vectorize a specific string property for a label. Once status is ready, every record matching that label+property pair is searchable via POST /api/v1/ai/search.
- [Overview](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/ai/overview): RushDB is a self-aware memory layer for agents, humans, and apps. It continuously understands its own structure — labels, fields, value distributions, relationships — and exposes that knowledge so agents can reason over real data without hallucinating schema details, and apps can retrieve semantically relevant context on demand.
- [Semantic Search](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/ai/search): POST /api/v1/ai/search
- [Writing Records with Vectors](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/ai/write-with-vectors): RushDB lets you attach pre-computed embedding vectors to records at write time, eliminating the need for a separate POST /api/v1/ai/indexes/:id/vectors/upsert call. Any endpoint that creates or modifies records accepts a vectors field.
- [Introduction](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/introduction): Base URL//api.rushdb.com/api/v1
- [Labels](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/labels): Search Labels
- [Properties](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/properties): Search Properties
- [Raw Queries](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/raw-queries): This endpoint is only available when your project is connected to your own Neo4j database. Connecting a custom Neo4j instance is available on the free tier — see the RushDB dashboard to set it up.
- [Create Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/records/create-records): Create a Record
- [Delete Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/records/delete-records): Delete by ID
- [Export Data](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/records/export-data): Export records as CSV. Accepts the same where / orderBy / skip / limit / labels parameters as a search query.
- [Get Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/records/get-records): Get Record by ID
- [Import Data](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/records/import-data): Import JSON
- [Update Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/records/update-records): Partial Update
- [Relationships](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/relationships): Attach
- [Transactions](https://docs.rushdb.com/rest-api/transactions): Begin Transaction
- [Agent-Safe Query Planning with Ontology First](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/agent-safe-query-planning): A repeatable agent pattern — ontology first, query spec second, constrained execution, and failure recovery when labels or fields are wrong.
- [Using RushDB Agent Skills in OpenClaw](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/agent-skills-with-openclaw): End-to-end guide — connect RushDB to OpenClaw, install the RushDB skills pack, and let your AI assistant query, store, and model structured data without writing a single line of code.
- [Semantic Search in 5 Minutes](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/ai-semantic-search): Create embedding indexes, wait for backfill, and run your first semantic search query in TypeScript, Python, or REST.
- [Audit Trails with Immutable Events and Derived State](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/audit-trails): Log business events as immutable records separate from current state so teams can reconstruct what happened, not just what is true now.
- [Building Team Memory for Product and Support Workflows](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/building-team-memory): Ingest tickets, docs, decisions, incidents, and feature requests into a connected graph so your team can retrieve context instead of isolated documents.
- [BYOC vs Managed vs Self-Hosted](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/byoc-vs-managed): Compare RushDB's three deployment models — Managed, BYOC (Bring Your Own Cloud), and Self-Hosted — with a feature matrix, decision guide, and step-by-step migration path.
- [Bring Your Own Vectors (BYOV) — External Embeddings](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/byov-external-embeddings): Use your own embedding model to generate vectors and store them in RushDB, then search with queryVector instead of query text.
- [BYOV in Practice: When and Why to Bring Your Own Vectors](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/byov-when-and-why): A case study showing exactly when BYOV makes sense, when it doesn't, and a complete walkthrough of a real scenario where you already have vectors from your own pipeline.
- [Choosing Relationship Types That Age Well](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/choosing-relationship-types): When to use generic nesting-driven edges versus explicit typed relationships, and how that choice affects readability, search, and analytics downstream.
- [Compliance and Retention Patterns](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/compliance-retention): Implement expiration, archival, and field-level redaction for GDPR, CCPA, and other data lifecycle requirements in RushDB without breaking graph structure.
- [Get API Key](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/configuring-dashboard): Register for RushDB, create a project, and generate your first API token to start building.
- [Connecting a Neo4j Aura Instance (BYOC)](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/connect-aura-instance): Use your own Neo4j or Aura database as the RushDB data store — keep data in your cloud account while using the full RushDB API.
- [Customer 360 as a Connected Graph](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/customer-360): Model users, accounts, subscriptions, invoices, touchpoints, and support interactions as a connected graph so customer context becomes retrievable instead of siloed.
- [End-to-End Data Lineage: From Source to Answer](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/data-lineage): Model imported records, transformation steps, derived summaries, and final outputs so every answer can be traced back to its upstream source.
- [Self-Hosting RushDB](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/deployment): Deploy RushDB on your own infrastructure with Docker Compose, connect your own Neo4j instance, and configure embedding support.
- [Discovery Queries: Exploring an Unknown Schema](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/discovery-queries): A practical workflow for exploring a RushDB project you did not design — using ontology tools, label listing, and progressive query refinement before building reliable retrieval.
- [Episodic Memory for Multi-Step Agents](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/episodic-memory): Store goals, intermediate observations, tool outputs, and decisions as linked records so long-running agents can resume with context instead of stateless prompts.
- [Event-Driven Ingestion from Webhooks and Queues](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/event-driven-ingestion): Handle partial, repeated, and out-of-order events from webhooks or message queues without corrupting connected graph state.
- [Explainable Results](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/explainable-results): Pair raw search results with related evidence, summary metrics, and traversal paths so users and agents can understand why a result was returned.
- [Building a Graph-Backed API Layer](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/graph-backed-api): Expose RushDB through an application API with query translation, safe filtering, and response shaping patterns for production use.
- [GraphRAG — Graph-Enriched Retrieval Augmented Generation](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/graphrag): Retrieve chunks semantically, then traverse the knowledge graph to assemble author, topic, and source provenance as richer LLM context.
- [Hybrid Retrieval: Filters Plus Semantic Search](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/hybrid-retrieval): Combine structured where-clause filtering with vector semantic search to narrow candidates by business constraints, then rank by relevance.
- [Importing data from external sources](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/importing-data): Learn how to import your data to RushDB
- [Importing from MongoDB](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/importing-from-mongodb): A step-by-step guide to migrating MongoDB collections to RushDB — nested documents, embedded arrays, upsert, and change streams
- [Incident Response Graphs](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/incident-response): Model operational incidents as graph structures to answer root cause, blast radius, and resolution timeline questions in a single query.
- [Tutorials](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/index): Step-by-step guides to build real things with RushDB — from deployment to AI-powered search and RAG pipelines.
- [Is RushDB Right for My Problem? A Practical Decision Guide](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/is-rushdb-right-for-me): A scenario-driven catalog showing seven problems RushDB is designed for, where it outperforms fragmented stacks, and where it isn't the right fit.
- [Local Setup](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/local-setup): Spin up a local RushDB instance with Docker Compose — no repository clone required.
- [MCP Quickstart for Real Operators: Claude, Cursor, and VS Code](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/mcp-operator-quickstart): Go beyond installation — learn the operator workflow for grounded, hallucination-resistant agent queries with the RushDB MCP server.
- [RushDB as a Memory Layer: Facts, Episodes, and References](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/memory-layer): Model persistent facts, episodic interactions, and linked reference material as a graph so agents and applications can retrieve and reason over connected context.
- [Modeling Hierarchies, Networks, and Feedback Loops](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/modeling-hierarchies): Three common graph shapes — trees, many-to-many networks, and cyclic systems — with guidance on how to query each without flattening away meaning.
- [Query Optimization](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/query-optimization): Shape SearchQuery, traversal breadth, aggregation strategy, and batch patterns to reduce compute cost and improve throughput.
- [RAG Evaluation](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/rag-evaluation): Measure Precision@k and Recall@k for your retrieval pipeline, detect score drift after model updates, and add a CI regression gate that fails on quality drops.
- [Multi-Source RAG](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/rag-multi-source): Ingest PDFs, web pages, and database records as distinct labels, then search across all sources in a single vector query with source-aware citations.
- [RAG Pipeline in Minutes](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/rag-pipeline): Chunk Markdown files, store them in RushDB, and build a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline in TypeScript, Python, or REST.
- [RAG Reranking](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/rag-reranking): Improve retrieval precision with two-stage search — over-fetch candidates with vector similarity, then rerank with LLM scoring or Reciprocal Rank Fusion before sending to the LLM.
- [Research Knowledge Graph: Papers, Authors, Topics, Citations](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/research-knowledge-graph): Build a scholarly graph supporting citation traversal, topical clustering, and author-centric discovery for research workflows.
- [Reusable SearchQuery](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/reusable-search-query): Learn the canonical SearchQuery shape reused across records, properties, labels, relationships, and values
- [Search UX Patterns](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/search-ux-patterns): Combine structured filters, semantic ranking, and contextual fields to build explainable, user-facing search experiences on top of RushDB.
- [SearchQuery Deep Dive: Advanced Patterns](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/searchquery-advanced-patterns): Build confidence with advanced SearchQuery patterns through realistic RushDB examples
- [Project Setup After Deployment](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/self-hosted-project-setup): Create projects, configure per-project embedding models, invite team members, and verify SDK connectivity on a self-hosted RushDB instance.
- [Semantic Search for Multi-Tenant Products](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/semantic-search-multitenant): Build tenant-safe semantic retrieval using RushDB's project-scoped prefilter and exact cosine similarity ranking — without global index assumptions.
- [Supply Chain Traceability and Recall Analysis](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/supply-chain-traceability): Model suppliers, batches, products, shipments, and incidents so teams can answer upstream-impact and downstream-blast-radius questions for recalls.
- [Temporal Graphs: Modeling State and Event Time Together](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/temporal-graphs): Represent durable entities alongside time-stamped events so you can answer both current-state and historical questions without losing lineage.
- [Testing SearchQuery Across TypeScript, Python, and REST](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/testing-searchquery): Write parity-driven tests that prove one query intent behaves identically across every RushDB surface.
- [Thinking in Graphs: From Tables to Traversals](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/thinking-in-graphs): Map the same product, customer, and order dataset from relational and document mental models into RushDB's graph model, then translate common business questions into multi-hop queries.
- [Webhook Ingestion from Third-Party Tools (Clay, Supabase, Superglue)](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/third-party-webhook-ingestion): A beginner-friendly guide to send JSON or CSV from no-code and data platforms into RushDB using custom HTTP requests.
- [Versioning Records Without Losing Queryability](https://docs.rushdb.com/tutorials/versioning-records): Compare in-place mutation, append-only versions, and hybrid versioning approaches — and how to query latest state while preserving historical analysis.
- [Advanced Indexing — BYOV](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/ai/advanced-indexing): External indexes (BYOV — Bring Your Own Vectors) let you supply pre-computed embedding vectors instead of having the server compute them. Use them when you need:
- [Embedding Indexes](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/ai/indexing): An embedding index is a policy that tells RushDB to vectorize a specific string property for a label. Once status is ready, every record matching that label+property pair is searchable via db.ai.search().
- [Overview](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/ai/overview): RushDB is a self-aware memory layer for agents, humans, and apps. It continuously understands its own structure — labels, fields, value distributions, relationships — and exposes that knowledge so that agents can reason over real data without hallucinating schema details, and apps can retrieve semantically relevant context on demand.
- [Semantic Search](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/ai/search): db.ai.search() performs semantic vector search across records that have an associated embedding index.
- [Writing Records with Vectors](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/ai/write-with-vectors): RushDB lets you attach pre-computed embedding vectors to records at write time, eliminating the need for a separate upsertVectors call. Any operation that creates or modifies records supports this through the vectors parameter.
- [Introduction](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/introduction): Push JSON, query by value or meaning, traverse graphs — from Node.js or the browser.
- [Labels](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/labels): List which labels exist in your project and how many records each has.
- [Models](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/models): A Model binds a label to a schema, giving you typed access to all record operations without ever repeating the label name.
- [Properties](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/properties): Inspect and manage field definitions across your project.
- [Raw Queries](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/raw-queries): This endpoint is only available when your project is connected to your own Neo4j database. Connecting a custom Neo4j instance is available on the free tier — see the RushDB dashboard to set it up.
- [Create Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/records/create-records): Three methods for writing flat records. For nested/graph data see Import Data.
- [Delete Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/records/delete-records): Delete by ID
- [Get Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/records/get-records): RushDB provides four read methods: look up by ID, find one, find unique, or run a full search query.
- [Import Data](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/records/import-data): When importing data into RushDB, choose the method that matches your data shape:
- [Update Records](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/records/update-records): Two methods for updating records: update() patches fields, set() replaces them all.
- [Relationships](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/relationships): Connect records into a graph. Relationships have a type and a direction.
- [Transactions](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/transactions): Group multiple operations into an atomic unit — all succeed or all roll back.
- [DBRecord](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/DBRecord): DBRecord is a type representing a database record in RushDB. It combines internal system properties with schema-defined data properties.
- [DBRecordInstance](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/DBRecordInstance): DBRecordInstance is a class that wraps a DBRecord and provides methods for manipulating it. This class serves as an interface for working with individual records in the database. It allows for updating properties and managing relationships.
- [DBRecordsArrayInstance](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/DBRecordsArrayInstance): DBRecordsArrayInstance is a class that manages an array of DBRecordInstance objects. It typically represents the result of a search query that returns multiple records.
- [DBRecordTarget](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/DBRecordTarget): DBRecordTarget is a type that represents a target record for operations like set, update, attach, detach, and delete. It is commonly used when working with relationships and property updates.
- [Model](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/Model): The Model class represents a schema-defined entity in RushDB. It provides methods to perform CRUD operations and manage relationships between records. Models are identified by labels in the database.
- [RelationTarget](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/RelationTarget): RelationTarget is a type that represents the target(s) for relationship operations like attach and detach.
- [RushDB](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/RushDB): The RushDB class is the main entry point for interacting with the RushDB database. It manages API connections and model registration. It provides access to records, labels, and transactions.
- [SearchQuery](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/SearchQuery): SearchQuery is a type that defines the structure for querying records in RushDB. It provides a flexible way to filter, sort, paginate, and compute metrics using select and groupBy. The legacy aggregate clause is deprecated and should only be used for vector similarity until select supports it. For more information on search concepts, see the search documentation.
- [Transaction](https://docs.rushdb.com/typescript-sdk/typescript-reference/Transaction): The Transaction class represents an active database transaction in RushDB. It provides methods for committing or rolling back the transaction and includes the unique transaction identifier.
